up.method: soft-sensor data reconciliation (C + degree-of-reduction balance) from measured off-gas (CER,OUR) and feed substrate

growth_records.cite: Mainka, Thomas et al. Soft Sensor-Based Monitoring and Efficient Control Strategies of Biomass Concentration for Continuous Cultures of Haloferax mediterranei and Their Application to an Industrial Production Chain. Microorganisms (2019). DOI:10.3390/microorganisms7120648. [PMC6956367]
growth_records.snippet: comparing experiments 1 and 2, where the glycerol concentration in the feed was decreased from 4 g L-1 to 2 g L-1 resulting in a decrease of biomass concentration from 8.4 g L-1 to 4.1 g L-1 ... r_X 8.01, r_S 12.35, X 8.4, Y_X/S 0.65 (Table
growth_records.notes: Table 1, Exp 1 (synthetic medium). Reported: R=0.74, s_gly=4 g/L, s_for=0, mu_set=0.026 /h, r_X=8.01 C-mmol/L/h, r_S=12.35 C-mmol/L/h, X_softsensor=8.4 g/L, Y_X/S=0.65 Cmol/Cmol. mu=(1-R)*D=(1-0.74)*0.1=0.026 /h (Eq 6). doubling_time=ln2/mu=0.693/0.026=26.66 h. Specific glycerol uptake qS = r_S/X/3(C per glycerol) = 12.35/8.4/3 = 0.490 mmol/gDW/h. CO2: C-balance r_S=CER+r_X -> CER=12.35-8.01=4.34 C-mmol/L/h; specific qCO2=4.34/8.4=0.517 mmol/gDW/h. r_S and r_X assumed +/-10% error per paper; reconciled soft-sensor values grounded in measured off-gas and feed substrate.

growth_records.cite: Mainka, Thomas et al. Soft Sensor-Based Monitoring and Efficient Control Strategies of Biomass Concentration for Continuous Cultures of Haloferax mediterranei and Their Application to an Industrial Production Chain. Microorganisms (2019). DOI:10.3390/microorganisms7120648. [PMC6956367]
growth_records.snippet: the glycerol concentration in the feed was decreased from 4 g L-1 to 2 g L-1 resulting in a decrease of biomass concentration from 8.4 g L-1 to 4.1 g L-1 (Table 1: r_X 3.83, r_S 5.99, X 4.1, Y_X/S 0.64)

growth_records.cite: Mainka, Thomas et al. Soft Sensor-Based Monitoring and Efficient Control Strategies of Biomass Concentration for Continuous Cultures of Haloferax mediterranei and Their Application to an Industrial Production Chain. Microorganisms (2019). DOI:10.3390/microorganisms7120648. [PMC6956367]
growth_records.snippet: formate was added in a concentration of 300 mg L-1 in experiments 2* and 3* ... Formate was completely degraded in all experiments (Table 1: r_X 4.18, r_S 6.87, X 4.2, Y_X/S 0.61)
growth_records.notes: Table 1, Exp 2* (glycerol 2 g/L + formate 0.3 g/L). r_X=4.18, r_S=6.87 C-mmol/L/h, X=4.2 g/L, Y_X/S=0.61, mu_set=0.026 /h. mu=(1-0.74)*0.1=0.026; td=26.66 h. qS total=6.87/4.2=1.636 C-mmol/gDW/h. Formate increment estimate = r_S(2*)-r_S(2)=6.87-5.99=0.88 C-mmol/L/h (approx, holds only if glycerol uptake unchanged). CER=6.87-4.18=2.69 C-mmol/L/h; qCO2=2.69/4.2=0.640 mmol/gDW/h. H. mediterranei cannot grow on formate as sole C source but co-degrades it with glycerol; formate fully removed.

growth_records.cite: Mainka, Thomas et al. Microorganisms (2019). DOI:10.3390/microorganisms7120648. [PMC6956367]
growth_records.snippet: Both aim for the same biomass concentration of 4.1 g L-1, but the Bleed:Feed ratio for experiment 3 was only half compared to experiment 2 (Table 1: R 0.87, r_X 1.84, r_S 3.06, X 4.1, Y_X/S 0.60)

growth_records.snippet: formate was added in a concentration of 300 mg L-1 in experiments 2* and 3* ... Formate was completely degraded (Table 1: R 0.87, r_X 2.14, r_S 3.79, X 4.7, Y_X/S 0.56)
growth_records.notes: Table 1, Exp 3* (glycerol 1 g/L + formate 0.3 g/L). r_X=2.14, r_S=3.79 C-mmol/L/h, X=4.7 g/L, Y_X/S=0.56, mu_set=0.013 /h. mu=(1-0.87)*0.1=0.013; td=53.31 h. qS total=3.79/4.7=0.806 C-mmol/gDW/h. Formate increment estimate = r_S(3*)-r_S(3)=3.79-3.06=0.73 C-mmol/L/h (approx). CER=3.79-2.14=1.65 C-mmol/L/h; qCO2=1.65/4.7=0.351 mmol/gDW/h. Formate fully degraded.

up.units: OUR measured online (Fig 4C), numeric not tabulated
up.method: off-gas analysis; yield Y_O2/S=0.60 mol O2/Cmol substrate

growth_records.cite: Mainka, Thomas et al. Soft Sensor-Based Monitoring and Efficient Control Strategies of Biomass Concentration for Continuous Cultures of Haloferax mediterranei and Their Application to an Industrial Production Chain. Microorganisms (2019). DOI:10.3390/microorganisms7120648. [PMC6956367]
growth_records.snippet: Each step was performed at two different specific growth rate levels (0.013 and 0.026 h-1) ... A higher mu leads to higher biomass yields Y_X/S, lower CO2 yields Y_CO2/S, and lower O2 yields Y_O2/S. (Table 2: mu=0.013: Y_O2/S 0.60, Y_CO2/S
growth_records.notes: Table 2 (industrial medium), mu=0.013 /h level. Reported yields reconciled along C and DoR balance: Y_O2/S=0.60+/-0.01 mol/Cmol, Y_CO2/S=0.46+/-0.01 Cmol/Cmol, Y_X/S=0.54+/-0.01 Cmol/Cmol. mu=0.013 /h reported (=(1-R)*D at R=0.87, D=0.1); td=0.693/0.013=53.31 h. Industrial brine = polyurethane process water 150 g/L NaCl + 200 mg/L formate, supplemented with synthetic-medium minerals (except NaCl) + glycerol 2-10 g/L. Biomass held 5-20 g/L across four steps; C-limited. Per-level specific uptake/CER/OUR shown only in Fig 4C/Fig 5 (linear r_S correlations), not numerically tabulated -> rate=null. Formate (200 mg/L) fully degraded.

growth_records.cite: Mainka, Thomas et al. Soft Sensor-Based Monitoring and Efficient Control Strategies of Biomass Concentration for Continuous Cultures of Haloferax mediterranei and Their Application to an Industrial Production Chain. Microorganisms (2019). DOI:10.3390/microorganisms7120648. [PMC6956367]
growth_records.snippet: A higher mu (0.026 h-1) leads to higher biomass yields Y_X/S, lower CO2 yields Y_CO2/S, and lower O2 yields Y_O2/S compared to lower mu (0.013 h-1). (Table 2: mu=0.026: Y_O2/S 0.55, Y_CO2/S 0.40, Y_X/S 0.60)
growth_records.notes: Table 2 (industrial medium), mu=0.026 /h level. Yields (C+DoR reconciled): Y_O2/S=0.55+/-0.01 mol/Cmol, Y_CO2/S=0.40+/-0.01 Cmol/Cmol, Y_X/S=0.60+/-0.01 Cmol/Cmol. mu=0.026 /h reported (=(1-R)*D at R=0.74); td=0.693/0.026=26.66 h. Same industrial brine as low-mu record. Per-level absolute uptake/CER/OUR only in figures (Fig 4C/5), not tabulated -> rate=null. Formate 200 mg/L fully degraded; A comparison of yields for synthetic and industrial medium did not show significant differences.

growth_records.medium: Minimal synthetic medium (MSM) with C4:0/C5:0 VFA mix, 0.1 M carbon

up.method: HPLC (Aminex HPX-87H) delta-VFA over 24 h; DeltaS=[VFA]feed-[VFA]outlet

sec.method: GC-FID on extracted polymer; steady-state productivity 77.5 mg/L/h x 1.2 L wv approx

yields.product_yields: Y_X/S = 0.07 g biomass/g substrate; Y_PHA/S = 0.18 g PHA/g substrate; overall PHBV productivity 9.6 mg/L/h; productivity normalized by OD 0.87 mg/L/h/OD

growth_records.cite: Parroquin-Gonzalez, Mariana et al. Continuous bioreactor production of polyhydroxyalkanoates in Haloferax mediterranei. Frontiers in Bioengineering and Biotechnology (2023). DOI:10.3389/fbioe.2023.1220271. [PMC10534070]
growth_records.snippet: Exp A: 0.1 M carbon, dilution rate 0.0044 h-1, flowrate out 5.3 mL/h, VFA consumption 54.1 mg/h (C4:0 27.2, C5:0 26.9). PHBV 1.86 g/L, overall productivity 9.6 mg/L/h, steady-state production rate 9.9 mg/h, Yx/s 0.07, Ypha 0.18.
growth_records.notes: Experiment A (continuous/chemostat), Tables 1 & 2. Haloferax mediterranei is a halophilic ARCHAEON (prokaryote). Growth rate taken as mu=D=0.0044 h-1 at steady state (dilution rate 'selected as expected specific growth rate'); doubling_time=ln2/mu=0.693/0.0044=157.5 h (very slow because continuous outlet removes cells, limiting net growth). Uptake in mg/h whole-reactor (1.2 L); volumetric = 54.1/1.2 = 45.1 mg/L/h. mmol conversions shown per compound (butyric MW 88.11, valeric MW 102.13). Cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h: paper does not report a numeric steady-state DCW for continuous runs (biomass only via OD10-12 and Yx/s). PHBV is intracellular accumulated product, listed under secretion as product-formation rate.

growth_records.medium: Minimal synthetic medium (MSM) with C4:0/C5:0 VFA mix, 0.25 M carbon

up.method: HPLC (Aminex HPX-87H) delta-VFA over 24 h; DeltaS=[VFA]feed-[VFA]outlet

sec.method: GC-FID on extracted polymer; steady-state productivity 71.3 mg/L/h

yields.product_yields: Y_X/S = 0.32 g biomass/g substrate; Y_PHA/S = 0.51 g PHA/g substrate; overall PHBV productivity 17.2 mg/L/h; productivity normalized by OD 1.43 mg/L/h/OD

growth_records.cite: Parroquin-Gonzalez, Mariana et al. Continuous bioreactor production of polyhydroxyalkanoates in Haloferax mediterranei. Frontiers in Bioengineering and Biotechnology (2023). DOI:10.3389/fbioe.2023.1220271. [PMC10534070]
growth_records.snippet: Exp B: 0.25 M carbon, dilution rate 0.0076 h-1, flowrate out 9.2 mL/h, VFA consumption 30.9 mg/h (C4:0 22.3, C5:0 8.6). PHBV 1.71 g/L, overall productivity 17.2 mg/L/h, steady-state production rate 15.8 mg/h, Yx/s 0.32, Ypha 0.51.
growth_records.notes: Experiment B (continuous/chemostat), Tables 1 & 2. Growth rate mu=D=0.0076 h-1 at steady state; doubling_time=ln2/0.0076=91.2 h. Faster-growing culture (favors C4:0 over C5:0 consumption). Uptake mg/h whole-reactor (1.2 L); volumetric=30.9/1.2=25.75 mg/L/h. mmol conversions per compound shown. Higher yields than Exp A (Yx/s 0.32 vs 0.07). Note: Table 2 also lists two FED-BATCH rows (Yx/s 0.29 / Ypha 0.73 etc.) that are CITED from Parroquin Gonzalez & Winterburn 2022 (ref 35), not original to this paper, and are therefore not emitted as separate records.

derived.qs_mu_note: pooled across studies/media — not a single chemostat series, so no maintenance model fitted
